article_title: How to Build a Daily Web Scraper with Claude Code
core_thesis: This article provides a step-by-step guide to building an automated daily web scraper using Claude Code, Playwright, and GitHub Actions, using Amazon product pages as a demo. It argues that AI-powered web browsing has recently become reliable enough for practical automation, and that Playwright scripts are superior to Claude in Chrome for repetitive scraping tasks due to speed and reliability. The authors emphasize that this approach saves significant time for anyone who regularly monitors website data, such as e-commerce sellers tracking product ratings or badges.
detailed_summary: The article, co-authored by Daria Cupareanu and Alex Willen, walks through building a daily web scraper that monitors Amazon product pages for changes in ratings, average rating, badges, and variant separation. Alex Willen, a former B2B SaaS PM who now runs 11 Amazon brands, explains his motivation: Amazon does not expose certain data (like badge changes or variant separation) via APIs, so scraping is necessary. He compares two approaches: Claude in Chrome (a browser extension that takes screenshots and interprets them) and Playwright (a browser automation framework that interacts with page code). For repetitive scraping, Playwright is recommended because it is faster, more reliable, and can run without Claude's active involvement once the script is written. The article then details the implementation: install Playwright and its MCP, decide what to scrape (ratings count, average rating), store data in a simple .txt file, set up Gmail alerts via GitHub Actions, and schedule the workflow to run daily using a local GitHub Actions runner (to avoid Amazon's IP blocking of GitHub-hosted runners). The authors also discuss limitations: Playwright scripts are fragile if page structure changes, CAPTCHAs can break automation, and monitoring for failures is important (e.g., using GitHub Actions notifications or a separate Claude Code job). The article concludes with a call to action: pick one manual monitoring task and automate it using the described approach.

entities.name: Alex Willen
entities.role: Author and subject matter expert; former B2B SaaS PM who runs 11 Amazon brands and built the scraper described.

entities.name: Daria Cupareanu
entities.role: Author and publisher of the 'AI Blew My Mind' Substack newsletter.

entities.name: Claude Code
entities.role: AI coding assistant by Anthropic used to generate and orchestrate the scraping scripts.

entities.name: Playwright
entities.role: Browser automation framework used to programmatically scrape web pages.

entities.name: GitHub Actions
entities.role: CI/CD platform used to schedule and run the daily scraping workflow.

entities.name: Amazon
entities.role: Target website for the demo scraper; known for IP blocking and CAPTCHAs.

key_data_points: Playwright is recommended over Claude in Chrome for repetitive scraping because it is faster, more reliable, and does not require Claude's active decision-making during execution (🟢 relevant to your Red Teaming: understanding Playwright's strengths helps in automating reconnaissance or data exfiltration tasks).

Amazon blocks scraping from GitHub-hosted runner IPs, so a local GitHub Actions runner must be used instead (🟢 relevant to your homelab/DevOps: you need to configure a self-hosted runner for similar automation).

The scraper stores data in a simple .txt file for simplicity, but the author notes that for larger datasets, a SQLite database is preferable (🟢 relevant to your programming: choose storage based on query needs; .txt is fine for minimal data).

Gmail App Passwords are used for email alerts, but the author warns this is not the most secure option (🟢 relevant to your cybersecurity: consider using OAuth or a dedicated notification service for better security).

Playwright scripts are fragile: if the target page structure changes, the script will likely break. CAPTCHAs can also cause failures, though simple ones (e.g., button clicks) can be handled with additional logic (🟢 relevant to your Red Teaming: when building scrapers for target sites, anticipate anti-bot measures and build in fallback logic).

The author recommends monitoring the scraper's health via GitHub Actions notifications or a separate Claude Code job that checks if the data file was updated (🟢 relevant to your DevOps: implement health checks for automated workflows to detect silent failures).
methodology_steps: Install Playwright and Playwright Chromium, plus the Playwright MCP. Ask Claude to handle the installation commands.
Decide what to scrape (e.g., number of ratings, average rating), where to store data (e.g., .txt file), and how to notify (e.g., email via Gmail App Password).

Test the initial scrape: prompt Claude to write a Playwright script that visits one URL, extracts the data, and saves it to a local .txt file. Use the Playwright MCP for exploration, but have Claude write a standalone script for automation.
Set up Gmail alerts: generate a Gmail App Password, store it as a GitHub secret (using `gh secret set`), and provide the sender/receiver email addresses to Claude.
Test the comparison and alert logic: manually modify the .txt file to simulate changes and verify that an email is sent; then test with no changes to confirm no email is sent.
Schedule the workflow to run daily using GitHub Actions. Configure a self-hosted runner on your local machine to avoid IP blocking by Amazon.
Optionally, set up a separate monitoring job (e.g., another Claude Code script) to verify that the scraper ran successfully each day.
conclusions: AI-powered web browsing has recently become reliable enough for practical automation, and Playwright scripts are the best choice for repetitive scraping tasks due to speed and reliability. This conclusion is supported by the author's successful daily use of the scraper for his Amazon brands.

The main limitations of this approach are script fragility (page changes break the script) and CAPTCHAs. For critical workflows, the author suggests using Claude Code with the Playwright MCP in a scheduled job, though this is more token-intensive. This is relevant to your Red Teaming: when automating data collection from target sites, you must plan for anti-bot measures and page structure changes.

Monitoring the scraper's health is essential because silent failures (e.g., script breaks but no email sent) can go unnoticed. The article recommends using GitHub Actions notifications or a separate monitoring job. This is relevant to your DevOps: always implement health checks for automated systems.
personal_relevance_summary: This article is highly relevant to your interests in programming, homelabbing, and cybersecurity. As a programmer, you get a concrete example of using Claude Code and Playwright to automate web scraping, which you can adapt for your own projects (e.g., monitoring bug bounty targets or tracking changes on forums). For your homelab/DevOps interests, the article covers setting up a self-hosted GitHub Actions runner, storing secrets, and scheduling workflows—all practical skills. From a Red Teaming perspective, the discussion of Playwright's fragility and CAPTCHA handling is directly applicable to building reliable reconnaissance tools. The article also provides a clear methodology for automating repetitive tasks, which aligns with your ambition to take initiative and build efficient workflows.
extra_advice: Consider extending this scraper to monitor security-relevant sites, such as CVE databases or bug bounty program pages, for changes. You could also add a webhook to trigger alerts to a Discord or Slack channel instead of email, which is more real-time and integrates with your existing workflow. Additionally, experiment with using the Playwright MCP for ad-hoc scraping of dynamic pages that require mid-stream decision-making, and compare its reliability against pre-written scripts for your specific use cases.
